formalChannels.title: Formal Channels (Regulated Rails)
formalChannels.description: Licensed commercial banks, money transfer operators (Wise, Western Union, MoneyGram), and licensed fintechs. These routes operate under central bank supervision, AML/CFT compliance audits, and clear state jurisdictions.

stats.label: Regulatory Considerations
stats.value: Strict KYC, compliance verification, and transaction tracking to prevent financial crimes.

formalChannels.footerNote: Provides full legal protection, deposit insurance, and transaction receipts.

informalChannels.title: Informal Channels (Hawala / Unlicensed Networks)
informalChannels.description: Trust-based systems operating outside formal bank supervision. Money does not physically cross borders. Instead, Hawaladars net off balances locally through international trade invoices, gold trading, and familial ties.

stats.label: Operational Risk
stats.value: High counterparty risk (the agent may abscond). High risk of severe criminal penalties.

informalChannels.footerNote: Purely trust-based. Operates in regions with blocked banking channels or high inflation.

railControlCards.title: Central Banks
railControlCards.description: Operate large-value national payment rails (e.g. Fedwire, TARGET2) and set exchange rate rules.

railControlCards.title: Commercial Banks
railControlCards.description: Hold international liquidity in correspondent accounts and handle the legacy SWIFT messaging routing.

railControlCards.title: Payment Networks
railControlCards.description: Card schemes (Visa, MasterCard) clearing payments on proprietary international rails.

railControlCards.title: Fintech & Stablecoins
railControlCards.description: Provide aggregations or block public ledger protocols directly, bypassing legacy banking loops.

whyMattersCards.title: Financial Inclusion
whyMattersCards.content: Remittances are often the first step into formal banking for rural families, enabling saving accounts and micro-insurance.

whyMattersCards.title: Macroeconomic Stability
whyMattersCards.content: In many developing nations, remittance flows exceed foreign direct investment (FDI) and official development aid combined, contributing up to 30% of national GDP.

whyMattersCards.title: Family Support
whyMattersCards.content: Direct cross-border support goes straight to households, financing daily necessities, education, healthcare, and micro-businesses.

sdgNote: UN Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 10.c: Aims to reduce the transaction costs of migrant remittances to less than 3% and eliminate corridor costs higher than 5% by 2030. High fee margins directly extract capital from vulnerable families.
